feedback.text: Dear members of the EU commission . The Digital Fairness Act as it stands now is definitly a first step in the right direction and I wholeheartedly applaud the EU commission and it's members for this Act. That beeing said, the scope of videogames could and should, in my humble opinion, be expanded upon. The videogame industry amasses billions in revenue worldwide. Yet the industry is riddled with egregious anti-consumer practices. The dark patterns in gaming have been there for a long while and it is good to see the EU take this matter seriously. It is not only important to protect children, but also gambling addicts or consumers who are vulnerable to that kind of behaviour. Big publishers, often publicly traded companies with a net worth of billions, are treating consumers as second class citizens. Exploitation of consumers is the standard among these publishers these days. Especially in the form of literally stripping away videogames that they have already sold to consumers at a premium price. The reasoning the industry gives us is that it is unfeasible to support videogames forever when they are no longer commercially viable, yet this is not what we as consumers demand from said publishers. Allow me to redirect your attention to the ECI Stop Destroying Videogames which has already garnered support from over 1.4 million EU citizens. The industry resorts to adding unacceptable clauses in their EULA's. EULA's you as a consumer have no access to untill AFTER you have purchased the product. They reserve the right to remove your purchase completely for any reason or no reason at all. In most cases you find these anti-consumer clauses somewhere at the back of the EULA, which sometimes has over a 100 pages. And to be fair,not a single consumer reads a EULA. They buy a game and they want to start playing without having to go through a hundred pages of "legal speak". There is a lack of disclosure up front, prohibiting consumers to make informed purchasing decisions. Games are almost exclusively sold through means of a one time payment, yet EULA's claim you have not bought a product, but you have effectively bought a limited license. Yet storefronts use wording as "BUY NOW" instead of "rent license" to obfuscate the publisher's true intent. Planned obscolesence runs rampant in the industry, which in my humble opinion is another example of "dark patterns". Publishers will effectively deny access to a title you have bought, just to incentivize you to buy their new upcoming titles. Older titles can no longer be bought as the publishers delist these older titles. (artificial scarcity) Everything is motivated by greed and we would implore the EU commission to step in to stop these predatory practices. Then we have the issue of censorship: Payment processors (VISA/Mastercard) forcing storefronts to delist legal fictional products under the guise of protecting the VISA/Mastercard brand. It is basicly "comply or we deny you service". The only reason VISA/Mastercard gets away with this is because of their strong position in the market. It is literally a duopoly. Payment processors influence should be limited to process payments and should not UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCE be allowed to censor legal works of art. Payment processors are NOT a governing body and should not try to act like one. I thank you for reading my feedback. With high regards.
